title: Operating Systems — x86 ASM/Kernel Labs 2–8
summary: A comprehensive x86 32-bit OS implementation course spanning Labs 2–8. Students implement assembly routines, C/C++/ASM interop, kernel utilities (stdlib, linked list, bitmap, address pool), and kernel subsystems (VGA stdio, interrupt management, round-robin thread scheduling, synchronization primitives, and virtual memory management). Each module's implementation is replicated across up to 19 lab exercise snapshots; the same logic must be filled in for every copy.
instructionPreview: This is a comprehensive task from an undergraduate operating-systems course. The project spans Labs 2–8 and covers x86 assembly programming, OS utility data structures, kernel I/O, interrupt handling, thread management, synchronization primitives, and memory management. You must implement all stubbed-out functions (marked with `/* TODO: implement */` or bare assembly labels) across the entire codebase so that all tests pass.
instruction:
This is a comprehensive task from an undergraduate operating-systems course.
The project spans Labs 2–8 and covers x86 assembly programming, OS utility data structures,
kernel I/O, interrupt handling, thread management, synchronization primitives, and memory
management. You must implement all stubbed-out functions (marked with `/* TODO: implement */`
or bare assembly labels) across the entire codebase so that all tests pass.

Your working directory is `/workspace`. The project code is already present.

## Modules to Implement

The following modules need to be implemented or substantially built out:

- **Lab 2 — x86 Assembly Routines** (`lab2/assignment/`): Three 32-bit x86 NASM assembly
  routines called by a C++ test harness using the cdecl calling convention. These cover
  branching, looping with C function calls, and null-terminated string iteration. (~1 file,
  3 functions to implement)

- **Lab 4 — C/C++/Assembly Interop** (`lab4/src/4/`): Demonstrates calling between C, C++,
  and assembly. The assembly function must call the C and C++ functions in turn; each function
  prints an identifying message. (~3 files, 3 functions to implement)

- **Utils — Doubly-Linked List** (`lab5–lab8 src/*/src/utils/list.cpp`): A doubly-linked
  list data structure used throughout the OS kernel for ready queues, waiting lists, and
  process tracking. Supports push/pop front/back, positional insert/erase, find, and
  indexed access. The same List class is used in all labs from Lab 5 onward. (~16 copies,
  14 methods each)

- **Utils — Standard Library** (`lab5–lab8 src/*/src/utils/stdlib.cpp`): Kernel-space utility
  functions including integer-to-string conversion for arbitrary bases (2–26) and a memory
  fill function. (~17 copies, 2 functions each)

- **Utils — Bitmap Allocator** (`lab7–lab8 src/*/src/utils/bitmap.cpp`): A bitmap-based
  resource allocator that tracks allocated/free resources as individual bits. Supports
  initialization, get/set individual bits, allocating contiguous free regions, and releasing
  allocated regions. (~11 copies, 5 methods each)

- **Utils — Address Pool** (`lab7–lab8 src/*/src/utils/address_pool.cpp`): A page-granularity
  address allocator built on top of BitMap. Manages pools of physical or virtual pages with
  a configurable start address. (~11 copies, 3 methods each)

- **Kernel — Screen I/O (stdio)** (`lab4–lab8 src/*/src/kernel/stdio.cpp`): VGA text-mode
  screen output, cursor management, screen scrolling, string printing, and printf-style
  formatted output with support for %d, %x, %c, %s, and %% format specifiers. (~18 copies,
  ~8 functions each)

- **Kernel — Interrupt Management** (`lab4–lab8 src/*/src/kernel/interrupt.cpp`): Interrupt
  Descriptor Table (IDT) entry setup, 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller initialization,
  time interrupt enable/disable, and the clock interrupt handler that drives preemptive
  scheduling. (~19 copies, ~5 functions each)

- **Kernel — Thread/Process Management** (`lab5–lab8 src/*/src/kernel/program.cpp`): Thread
  creation with stack frame setup, round-robin scheduler with time-slice handling, PCB
  allocation/deallocation, thread exit, and (in later labs) MESA-semantics wakeup for
  blocked threads. (~16 copies, ~5–6 functions each)

- **Kernel — Synchronization** (`lab6–lab8 src/*/src/kernel/sync.cpp`): SpinLock using
  atomic exchange for busy-waiting mutual exclusion, and Semaphore with counter-based
  blocking/waking using the SpinLock and thread waiting queue. (~14 copies, 3 functions each)

## Test-Coupled Signatures

The following functions are called directly by the test suite. You must implement
them with these exact signatures and behaviors:

- `your_if` (x86 NASM label in `lab2/assignment/student.asm`) — sets extern int `if_flag`
  based on the value of extern int `a1` using conditional branching
- `your_while` (x86 NASM label in `lab2/assignment/student.asm`) — loops while `a2 >= 12`,
  calling `my_random()` and storing results into `while_flag[a2-12]`, then decrementing `a2`
- `your_function` (x86 NASM label in `lab2/assignment/student.asm`) — iterates over
  `your_string` calling `print_a_char(char c)` for each character via cdecl convention
- `function_from_asm` (x86 NASM in `lab4/src/4/asm_utils.asm`) — calls `function_from_C()`
  and `function_from_CPP()` in sequence
- `void function_from_C()` (in `lab4/src/4/c_func.c`) — prints "This is a function from C."
- `void function_from_CPP()` (in `lab4/src/4/cpp_func.cpp`) — prints "This is a function
  from C++."
- `void List::initialize()` (in `src/utils/list.cpp`) — resets the list to empty state
- `int List::size()` — returns number of elements in the list
- `bool List::empty()` — returns true if the list has no elements
- `ListItem *List::front()` — returns pointer to the first element, or nullptr if empty
- `ListItem *List::back()` — returns pointer to the last element, or nullptr if empty
- `void List::push_back(ListItem *itemPtr)` — appends an element to the end
- `void List::pop_back()` — removes the last element
- `void List::push_front(ListItem *itemPtr)` — prepends an element to the front
- `void List::pop_front()` — removes the first element
- `void List::insert(int pos, ListItem *itemPtr)` — inserts an element at position pos
- `void List::erase(int pos)` — removes the element at position pos
- `void List::erase(ListItem *itemPtr)` — removes a specific element by pointer
- `ListItem *List::at(int pos)` — returns pointer to element at position pos
- `int List::find(ListItem *itemPtr)` — returns position of element, or -1 if not found
- `void itos(char *numStr, uint32 num, uint32 mod)` (in `src/utils/stdlib.cpp`) — converts
  a non-negative integer to a string in the specified base (2–26), with high-order digit
  first; digits above 9 use uppercase letters A–P
- `void memset(void *memory, char value, int length)` (in `src/utils/stdlib.cpp`) — fills
  length bytes starting at memory with value
- `void BitMap::initialize(char *bitmap, const int length)` (in `src/utils/bitmap.cpp`) —
  sets up the bitmap with the given storage and total bit count, clearing all bits to free
- `bool BitMap::get(const int index) const` — returns true if the resource at index is allocated
- `void BitMap::set(const int index, const bool status)` — sets bit at index to allocated
  (true) or free (false)
- `int BitMap::allocate(const int count)` — allocates count contiguous free bits, returns
  the index of the first, or -1 if not possible
- `void BitMap::release(const int index, const int count)` — frees count bits starting at
  index
- `void AddressPool::initialize(char *bitmap, const int length, const int startAddress)`
  (in `src/utils/address_pool.cpp`) — initializes the pool with bitmap storage, page count,
  and base address
- `int AddressPool::allocate(const int count)` — allocates count contiguous pages, returns
  the start address or -1
- `void AddressPool::release(const int address, const int amount)` — frees amount pages
  starting at address

## Build and Test

The lab2 assignment can be built with:
```
cd /workspace/lab2/assignment && make run
```

The lab4 interop example can be built with:
```
cd /workspace/lab4/src/4 && make
```

moduleDag.description: A comprehensive x86 32-bit OS implementation course spanning Labs 2–8. Students implement assembly routines, C/C++/ASM interop, kernel utilities (stdlib, linked list, bitmap, address pool), and kernel subsystems (VGA stdio, interrupt management, round-robin thread scheduling, synchronization primitives, and virtual memory management). Each module's implementation is replicated across up to 19 lab exercise snapshots; the same logic must be filled in for every copy.

nodes.label: Lab2 — x86 Assembly Routines
nodes.description: Three 32-bit x86 NASM routines (your_if, your_while, your_function) called by a C++ test harness via cdecl. Covers conditional branching, loop-with-C-callback, and null-terminated string iteration. Standalone — no kernel dependencies.

nodes.label: Lab4 — C/C++/Assembly Interop
nodes.description: Demonstrates calling between 32-bit NASM, C, and C++. The assembly entry point (function_from_asm) calls function_from_C() then function_from_CPP() in sequence; each prints an identifying message. Standalone — independent of all kernel modules.

nodes.label: Utils — Kernel Standard Library
nodes.description: Kernel-space utility functions: itos() converts a non-negative integer to a string in any base 2–26 (digits above 9 use uppercase letters A–P), memset() fills a byte range, and ceil() performs ceiling integer division. No dependencies on other custom modules. Appears in 17 copies across lab exercises.

nodes.label: Utils — Doubly-Linked List
nodes.description: A doubly-linked List used throughout the kernel for ready queues, waiting queues, and process tracking. Implements initialize, size, empty, front/back, push/pop front/back, positional insert/erase, pointer-based erase, at (by index), and find (by pointer). No dependencies on other custom modules. Appears in 16 copies.

nodes.label: Kernel — VGA Screen I/O (STDIO)
nodes.description: VGA text-mode screen output: absolute character placement at (x,y) in the 0xb8000 video buffer, hardware cursor management via I/O ports, one-line scroll, sequential character printing with automatic newline, and printf-style formatted output supporting %d, %x, %c, %s, %%. Uses itos() from utils_stdlib for numeric formatting. Appears in 18 copies.

nodes.label: Utils — BitMap Resource Allocator
nodes.description: Bitmap-based resource allocator: initialize() clears all bits using ceil() from stdlib; get/set read or write individual bits; allocate(count) finds and reserves the first run of count contiguous free bits (first-fit); release(index, count) frees a run. Foundation for utils_address_pool. Appears in 11 copies.

nodes.label: Kernel — Interrupt Management
nodes.description: IDT gate descriptor setup (setInterruptDescriptor), 8259A dual-PIC initialization via ICW1–4 and OCW1 port sequences, time-interrupt enable/disable by masking/unmasking IRQ0, and the clock interrupt handler (c_time_interrupt_handler) that writes a tick counter to the VGA screen via stdio. Appears in 19 copies.

nodes.label: Utils — Address Pool
nodes.description: Page-granularity address allocator wrapping BitMap. initialize() binds a bitmap buffer, page count, and base address; allocate(count) calls BitMap::allocate and converts the bit index to a byte address; release(addr, n) converts back to a bit index and calls BitMap::release. Appears in 11 copies.

nodes.label: Kernel — Thread/Process Management
nodes.description: Thread creation with initial stack-frame setup (executeThread), round-robin preemptive scheduler (schedule) invoked from the clock ISR, PCB allocation from a fixed pool, thread exit and PCB reclamation, and MESA-semantics wakeup (MESA_WakeUp) for semaphore-blocked threads added in later labs. Uses List for readyPrograms and allPrograms queues. Appears in 16 copies.

nodes.label: Kernel — Memory Management
nodes.description: Physical and virtual page allocator using AddressPool instances for kernel and user address spaces. Implements allocatePhysicalPages/releasePhysicalPages via address pools, allocateVirtualPages for virtual address space, allocatePages which maps virtual to physical pages by installing page-table entries, and openPageMechanism to enable x86 paging via CR0/CR3. Appears in 10 copies.

nodes.label: Kernel — Synchronization Primitives
nodes.description: SpinLock using hardware XCHG for busy-wait mutual exclusion, and Semaphore with a counter and a List waiting queue. Semaphore::P() blocks the calling thread when the counter is zero by appending to the waiting list and calling programManager.schedule(); Semaphore::V() increments the counter and calls programManager.MESA_WakeUp() on the first waiter. Appears in 14 copies.

edges.label: kernel_stdio calls itos() from utils_stdlib to format %d/%x arguments in printf
utils_bitmap calls ceil() from utils_stdlib to compute byte count from bit count in initialize()
c_time_interrupt_handler() uses the global stdio object to write tick output to the VGA screen
AddressPool wraps a BitMap member; all allocate/release operations delegate to BitMap
ProgramManager holds List readyPrograms and allPrograms; the scheduler pops/pushes PCBs via List
preemptive scheduling fires on every clock tick; c_time_interrupt_handler() calls programManager.schedule()
Semaphore holds a List waiting queue; V() calls waiting.front()/pop_front and push_back on the list
Semaphore::P() calls programManager.schedule() to yield the CPU; Semaphore::V() calls programManager.MESA_WakeUp()
MemoryManager holds AddressPool instances for kernel/user physical and virtual pages; allocatePages delegates to them
